items.question: State the goal of PCA in one sentence.
items.answer: PCA finds orthogonal linear directions that capture the most variance in centered data and projects samples onto the top k of those directions to reduce dimensionality while retaining as much spread as possible.

items.question: How are principal components related to the covariance matrix and SVD?
items.answer: They are eigenvectors of the sample covariance matrix C ordered by eigenvalues, equivalently right singular vectors of centered X from X̃ = U Σ Vᵀ; eigenvalues λᵢ and σᵢ²/(n−1) rank explained variance.

items.question: What is explained variance ratio and how is it used?
items.answer: For component i with eigenvalue λᵢ, the ratio λᵢ / Σⱼ λⱼ is the fraction of total variance that component explains; summing the first k ratios guides truncation and scree/elbow decisions.

items.question: When would you apply StandardScaler before PCA?
items.answer: When features have different units or scales and you want correlation-style PCA where no single high-variance feature dominates—standardizing sets each column to unit variance before decomposition.

items.question: Why can PCA hurt supervised performance after aggressive truncation?
items.answer: PCA maximizes unsupervised variance, not label information; low-variance components can carry class signal, so dropping them may remove predictive directions even if reconstruction variance looks acceptable.
